title: [source] A new log’s final asset references are set from the raw `asset` query parameter,

given: A request opens an add form for a new log.
The request has an `asset` query parameter containing one or more asset IDs.
At least one requested asset is not viewable by the current user, or does not load.
when: The log entity form is prepared.
then: The log’s `asset` field is ultimately assigned the complete raw requested ID list.
The loaded/viewable-only append loop is overwritten rather than acting as the final filter.

why_non_obvious: The code appears to securely append only viewable loaded assets, but the subsequent unconditional `set('asset', $asset_ids)` overwrites that field with every requested ID. A port based on the apparent intent or method names would likely retain only authorized assets.

source_read.rule: A new log’s final asset references are set from the raw `asset` query parameter, including IDs that failed the preceding view-access check; the earlier access-filtered appends do not determine the final field value.

title: [source] Cloning a log creates a distinct duplicate of every referenced quantity and rewr

given: A log has one or more referenced quantities.
when: The log is cloned.
then: The clone references newly duplicated quantity entities, one for each original referenced quantity, rather than the original entities.

why_non_obvious: A quantity field is an entity-reference field, so a straightforward clone implementation could copy its reference IDs. The clone event instead explicitly duplicates each child entity before assigning the references.

source_read.rule: Cloning a log creates a distinct duplicate of every referenced quantity and rewrites the cloned log's quantity list to those duplicates; it does not retain shared quantity references.

title: [source] Deleting a quantity removes that quantity's references from every log that refer

given: A quantity is referenced by one or more logs.
Some referencing logs may otherwise be inaccessible or in any lifecycle/status state.
when: The quantity is deleted.
then: Every matching log has only that quantity reference removed, and each such log receives and saves a new revision whose message identifies the deleted quantity UUID.

why_non_obvious: A reimplementation may assume dangling references are tolerated, update only active/published/visible logs, or silently mutate projections. The source queries all matching logs with access checks disabled, preserves other field values, and explicitly records a revision per affected log.

source_read.rule: Deleting a quantity removes that quantity's references from every log that references it, without access/status filtering, and each affected log is saved as a new revision with a deletion message.

title: [source] Submitting the add-log confirmation silently drops selected assets that the user

given: A user selected multiple assets for the “add a log” action.
At confirmation time, some selected assets are not viewable by that user.
At least one selected asset is viewable.
when: The user confirms and selects a log type.
then: The redirect to the new-log form contains only the IDs of the viewable assets.
No error or warning is produced for the omitted assets.

why_non_obvious: The originating action is authorized by update access, so an implementation might preserve every selected asset or check update access again; the confirmation step instead independently applies view access and treats a partial selection as valid.

source_read.rule: Submitting the add-log confirmation silently drops selected assets that the user cannot currently view, and proceeds with the remaining viewable assets rather than failing the whole operation.

title: [source] Deleting a log deletes every quantity currently referenced by its quantity field

given: A log has a quantity field containing referenced quantity entities.
when: The log is deleted.
then: The referenced quantity entities are deleted as well.

why_non_obvious: A field named quantity can plausibly be treated as a reusable measurement reference. The deletion hook implements an ownership-like cascade instead of merely removing references.

source_read.rule: The default quantity type is resolved by strict fallback priority: a non-empty per-log-type setting first, then the literal `standard` whenever the standard module exists, then the first loaded quantity type, otherwise null.

title: [source] Asset query prepopulation applies only while creating a new log, never when edit

given: A request contains an `asset` query parameter.
The form is for an existing log, a non-add operation, or a non-log entity.
when: The entity form is prepared.
then: No asset references are prepopulated from the query parameter.

why_non_obvious: A generic-looking entity-form hook and an `asset` query parameter could suggest that links can alter any log form; the behavior is deliberately limited to the add operation and a new `LogInterface` instance.

source_read.rule: Asset query prepopulation applies only while creating a new log, never when editing an existing log or preparing any non-log entity.

title: [source] The pending asset selection is single-use per user: it is deleted after every co

given: The current user has an asset selection stored for the add-log confirmation flow.
when: The confirmation form is submitted, whether or not it yields a log-add redirect.
then: The user’s stored selection is deleted.
A later attempt cannot reuse that selection unless the action is invoked again.

why_non_obvious: The temporary selection is created by an action and may look like durable workflow state, but cleanup is unconditional and occurs outside the successful redirect branch.

source_read.rule: The pending asset selection is single-use per user: it is deleted after every confirmation-form submission, including cancellation, an unconfirmed submission, or a submission without a usable log type.

title: [source] A log can hold multiple revision-aware quantity references, not a single scalar

given: A log is being represented with quantity measurements.
when: Quantities are attached to the log.
then: The log may reference multiple quantity entities/revisions in its `quantity` field.

why_non_obvious: The base field is named singular `quantity`, which could lead a port to model it as one reference or an embedded immutable value. Its declared reference type and cardinality allow a list of quantity entity revisions.

source_read.rule: A log can hold multiple revision-aware quantity references, not a single scalar quantity value.

title: [cm/access-check] access

given: an actor and a resource with a server-side access rule
when: the resource is read/written under the eventual, selective-disclosure model
then: the value visible to the actor matches the source's access decision

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this access-check site 'soft': [access-check] The call assumes a current server-side authorization decision for the update, but under eventual consistency replicas may temporarily disagree about permissions before access state converges, making this an eventually consistent access snapshot rather than a hard transactional invariant.

title: [cm/revision-lock] quantityDelete

given: a record two writers update from the same base revision
when: both updates are applied without a central lock
then: the converged value and the fate of the lost update are asserted

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this revision-lock site 'soft': [revision-lock] Setting the log entity to create a new revision is revision-history behavior, so concurrent updates may temporarily produce divergent revisions but the history can converge without requiring a lock.

title: [cm/access-check] entityPrepareForm

given: an actor and a resource with a server-side access rule
when: the resource is read/written under the eventual, selective-disclosure model
then: the value visible to the actor matches the source's access decision

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this access-check site 'soft': [access-check] The `access('view', $this->currentUser)` check assumes an up-to-date authorization state; under eventual consistency a stale ACL may transiently over- or under-permit viewing until synchronization, so the access result is eventually correct.

title: [domain-logic] LogQueryFactoryInterface

given: the domain state this class operates on
when: its primary operation is exercised
then: the value it computes / maintains is observed at the boundary

why_non_obvious: A high-reach domain class concentrates behavior many members depend on; its non-obvious rules are load-bearing for a faithful port.

title: [hook-implementation] UpdateHooks

given: the entity state the hook keys on
when: the lifecycle event that triggers the hook occurs
then: the hook's derived side effect is observable at the value boundary

why_non_obvious: A hook fires a side effect on an entity lifecycle event. A re-implementer who models only the primary write can miss the derived state the hook maintains.
